back: A tiered arrangement of memory types organized by speed, size, and cost. Fastest memory (registers) is at top, slowest (secondary storage) at bottom.
Recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again. Loop instructions and variables reuse exhibit temporal locality.
Items near recently accessed items are likely to be accessed soon. Sequential code execution and array access show spatial locality.
When the requested data is found in the cache memory. Results in fast access time (typically 1-2 clock cycles).
When requested data is not in cache and must be fetched from main memory. Results in higher latency (100+ cycles).
Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line using formula: Cache Line = (Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines). Simple but may cause conflicts.
Any memory block can be placed in any cache line. Most flexible but requires hardware comparison of all tags (expensive).
Combines direct and associative mapping. Cache is divided into sets, each containing multiple lines. Block maps to a set, but can be placed in any line within that set.
Data is written to both cache and main memory simultaneously. Simple, reliable, but slower writes. No cache/data inconsistency.
Data is written only to cache initially. Main memory is updated when cache line is replaced. Faster but can cause data inconsistency.
Ratio of cache hits to total memory accesses. Higher hit ratio = better performance. Typically 90-95% in well-designed systems.
Least Recently Used - evicts the cache line that hasn't been accessed for the longest time. Good temporal locality exploitation.

front: What is the purpose of Write Buffer?
back: A small queue that stores write requests before they go to main memory, allowing CPU to continue without waiting for slow memory writes.
Ensures that multiple caches (in multi-core systems) have consistent data. When one cache modifies data, others must be notified/invalidated.
